summary: Julia Evans argues against traditional goal-setting for learning programming, finding it stressful and unhelpful when goals go unmet. She proposes instead celebrating 'tiny milestones' — small learning achievements that happen naturally through projects and debugging. Using her own experience of setting C/C++ learning goals nine years ago and achieving none of them, she shows how she still made enormous progress in systems programming through a different path. The post reframes 'failed' goals as simply redirected learning, and encourages tracking and celebrating small wins to stay motivated.
key_points: Traditional learning goals can feel depressing when unmet, even when the reason you didn't pursue them is perfectly rational
Tiny milestones — like using a new tool, fixing a new type of bug, or learning a concept — are better markers of progress than big goals
Most meaningful learning happens 'by accident' through projects and bugs rather than explicit planning
Changing goals isn't failure; it reflects that your actual needs and interests evolved
Celebrating milestones somewhere (blog posts, brag documents) creates a tangible sense of progress that sustains motivation
money_quotes: Goals can be useful, but a lot of the time I actually find them stressful and not that helpful.
9 years later, I have done 0 of those things. With the "goal" framing, it's to think of this as a bad thing!
I find this framing depressing and unhelpful. In reality, I didn't have any real reason to learn C++ then and I still don't now.
Every time I solve a bug that I couldn't have solved before, I think of it as a tiny milestone.
The reason I still haven't learned C isn't that I suck or that C is impossible to learn. It's just that learning how to write C well was never actually something I had a real reason to do.
I think it's much more healthy to be flexible about your goals and to celebrate the milestones you do end up getting to instead of feeling bad about goals that you "failed" at.
Most of my tiny milestones came up naturally because I had a project I wanted to do or a bug I needed to solve.
key_insight: Tracking and celebrating small, naturally-occurring learning milestones is more motivating and realistic than setting big goals that may never match your actual needs.
